title: Resist felting a slipper form
subtitle: Using a bubble-wrap or foam resist to felt a three-dimensional slipper shape
excerpt: A resist is a flat template of bubble wrap or craft foam placed inside the wool lay to stop the two sides of a wet-felted piece from fusing together, creating a pocket you can open and wear. The resist-felting method is the foundation for slippers, bags, and any other hollow wet-felted form. This guide covers the slipper-shaped resist used for a simple pull-on slipper.
sourceNotes: Resist felting as a method for making hollow forms is established in Scandinavian and central-Asian felt traditions. The modern slipper construction method is taught widely in guild workshops and textile schools.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat waterproof template, usually bubble wrap or craft foam, placed inside a wet-felted piece to prevent the top and bottom layers from fusing. The resist is removed before final fulling to leave a hollow pocket.

In resist felting, folding the edges of the roving layer over the resist onto the opposite side, and then repeating on the other side, so each face of the finished form is covered by wool from both sides.

content.text: is a flat slipper-shaped template cut from bubble wrap or craft foam. It acts as the positive form inside the wool. Roving is laid around both sides of the resist, felted until the fabric locks, and then the resist is removed.
the edges over the resist joins the two faces seamlessly. Plan for 30-35% shrinkage from the resist template size to the finished slipper.

items.name: Bubble wrap or 3 mm craft foam
Hot water and cold water access

content.text: Draw a slipper outline on bubble wrap: trace around the foot, then add 35% to all dimensions to allow for shrinkage. The template should be a smooth, low-cut slip-on shape with no ankle construction. Cut two templates, one for each slipper.
Step 2: Lay roving on the first face

Place the resist on the bamboo mat. Lay three cross-hatched layers of roving over the resist, extending each layer about 2 cm beyond the resist edge on all sides except the opening (top). Wet out through the felting net and rub gently until the fibres begin to lock.
Step 3: Turn and lay the second face

Flip the resist over. Fold the overhanging roving edges from the first face onto this new face, pressing them flat. Lay three more cross-hatched layers on top of the folded edges to cover the second face fully. Wet out through the net and rub until this face begins to lock.
Step 4: Felt until firm, then remove the resist

Roll the slipper in the mat, applying pressure. Work both faces alternately. When the slipper has reduced by about half of its final planned shrinkage and the fabric holds its shape, carefully cut a small opening at the top of the slipper and ease the resist out. Do not tear the opening: the felt at this stage is still fragile.

Slide the damp slipper onto your foot (or a last if you have one). Work the slipper against a ribbed bath mat or the bamboo mat using your foot and hands: press, rub, and roll the fabric over your foot to full it to final shape. The slipper will conform to your foot as it shrinks. Once it fits snugly, rinse in cold water and dry on the foot or over a last.

items.problem: The two faces fuse together at the edge before the resist is removed
items.cause: Roving extended too far over the resist edge, fusing the front and back
items.fix: Keep the roving overhang to a maximum of 2 cm; trim any excess before wetting out.

items.problem: The opening tears when the resist is removed
items.cause: The felt is still too fragile at the cut point
items.fix: Continue felting until the fabric holds a pinch-and-release test before cutting. Cut with scissors, not hands.

items.problem: One slipper is larger than the other
items.cause: Different amounts of roving on each or uneven rolling pressure
items.fix: Weigh roving per slipper and roll each the same number of passes. Full both on the same foot last or use measurements.
